content.problemStatement:
Chef likes tournament graphs, which are directed graphs where each unordered pair of vertices is directly connected by exactly one edge. A directed graph is strongly connected if for each pair of vertices (a,b)(a, b)(a,b), there is a path from the vertex aaa to the vertex bbb.

Consider a tournament graph GGG with NNN vertices (numbered 111 through NNN). Chef takes a sequence of pairs of vertices (a1,b1),(a2,b2),…,(aM,bM)(a_1, b_1), (a_2, b_2), \ldots, (a_M, b_M)(a1​,b1​),(a2​,b2​),…,(aM​,bM​) and does the following for each iii from 111 to MMM in this order:


Flip the direction of the edge between vertices aia_iai​ and bib_ibi​.
If the graph is strongly connected either before or after flipping this edge, declare GGG a bad tournament.
If GGG is never declared a bad tournament, Chef calls it a good tournament. Given the sequence of MMM edge flips, find a good tournament.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 4000
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains two integers N and M. The next M lines each contain two integers a_i and b_i describing the i‑th edge flip. All values are space‑separated.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output N‑1 lines. For i from 1 to N‑1, output N‑i space‑separated integers. The j‑th integer on line i is 1 if there is a directed edge from vertex i to vertex i+j, otherwise 0. Print a newline after each line. The outputs of consecutive test cases are concatenated without extra blank lines.

sampleTestCases.explanation: N=3, we output a transitive tournament: edges 1→2, 1→3, 2→3. The two flips are ignored because the construction is always good.
First test case N=4 produces three lines of all 1's. Second test case N=5 produces four lines of all 1's. Flips are irrelevant.

hidden.label: maximum boundary
hidden.expectedOutput: A sequence of 399 lines where line i contains (400-i) copies of "1" separated by spaces

solution.approach: A transitive tournament (total order) is never strongly connected: all edges go from a lower‑indexed vertex to a higher‑indexed one. Flipping any single edge in such a tournament cannot create strong connectivity, because at least one vertex (the smallest) will still have no incoming edges and at least one vertex (the largest) will still have no outgoing edges. Therefore, for every test case we can ignore the given flips and simply output the adjacency matrix of the transitive tournament. For vertex i (1‑based) and vertex j>i we output 1, otherwise 0. This construction satisfies the definition of a good tournament for any sequence of flips.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T·N^2) to produce the output (reading input is O(T·M)).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra besides the input buffer.

code.C:
#include <stdio.h>
int main(){int T; if(scanf("%d",&T)!=1) return 0; while(T--){int N,M; scanf("%d %d",&N,&M); for(int i=0;i<M;i++){int a,b; scanf("%d %d",&a,&b);} for(int i=1;i<N;i++){for(int j=i+1;j<=N;j++){printf("1"); if(j<N) printf(" ");} printf("\n");} } return 0;}
